coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen, sulphur co-doped graphene quantum dot: An excellent sensor for nitroexplosives
coredata.dc:creator: Mondal, Tapas Kumar
Dinda, Diptiman
Saha, Shyamal Kumar
coredata.dc:description: Study of sensing properties of nitroexplosives using luminescent carbon based quantum dots is an interesting area of research. Enhancement in sensing ability exploiting edge states and doped states in quantum dots is an elegant approach. In the present work, graphene quantum dots (GQD) are co-doped by nitrogen and sulphur to produce large number of localized energy levels near conduction band. Remarkable enhancement in fluorescence quenching effect compared to their individual doped states either by nitrogen or sulphur is observed using only 90μM solution of 2,4,6-trinitro phenol (TNP) via charge transfer among these doped states to detect trinitrophenol selectively. The detection limit has been calculated to about 19.05 ppb. The origin of this ultra high fluorescence quenching and low detection limit is the presence of electron rich edge states due to ‘N’ and ‘S’ dopants as verified by Zeta potential results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Co-doping
Sensing
PL quenching
PET mechanism,

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensing of nitroexplosives in aqueous medium
Synthesis of GQD, N-GQD, S-GQD, N, S-GQD

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis scheme of N,S-GQD from Graphene oxide.
(a), (b) TEM images of N,S-GQD; (c) FTIR spectra of N,S-GQD; (d) RAMAN spectra of N,S-GQD.
(a) Low range XPS spectra of N,S-GQD; (b) High resolution de-convoluted XPS peaks for C–1 s of N,S-GQD.
(a) Excitation dependent PL spectra of N,S-GQD solution; (b) pH dependent PL spectra of N,S-GQD.
(a) Fluorescence quenching of N,S-GQD after gradual addition of different amount of 1mM TNP solution at pH 7; (b)PL quenching efficiency of N,S-GQD toward different nitro aromatic compounds.
(a) Stern–Volmer plot for different nitro aromatic compounds; (b) Increase in the fluorescence quenching efficiency upon addition of different nitro compounds followed by 1 mM TNP solution at pH 7.
(a) Fluorescence quenching of N,S-GQD in presence of lower concentration of TNP; (b) Detection limit of TNP by N,S-GQD solution.

(a) Quenching efficiency of different quantum dots solution after addition of 200μL of 1mM TNP solution at pH 7; (b) Spectral overlap between emission spectrum of N,S-GQD and absorption spectra of different nitro aromatic compounds.
Fluorescence Quenching Constant (KS−V) Values for Different Nitro Explosives.
Overlap Integral J (λ) Values for Different Explosives.
Overlap Integral J (λ) Values for Different graphene quantum dots with TNP.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen, sulphur co-doped graphene quantum dot: An excellent sensor for nitroexplosives

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science, Indian Association for the Cultivation of Science Jadavpur, Kolkata, 700032, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Indian Association for the Cultivation of Science Jadavpur

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Study of sensing properties of nitroexplosives using luminescent carbon based quantum dots is an interesting area of research. Enhancement in sensing ability exploiting edge states and doped states in quantum dots is an elegant approach. In the present work, graphene quantum dots (GQD) are co-doped by nitrogen and sulphur to produce large number of localized energy levels near conduction band. Remarkable enhancement in fluorescence quenching effect compared to their individual doped states either by nitrogen or sulphur is observed using only 90μM solution of 2,4,6-trinitro phenol (TNP) via charge transfer among these doped states to detect trinitrophenol selectively. The detection limit has been calculated to about 19.05 ppb. The origin of this ultra high fluorescence quenching and low detection limit is the presence of electron rich edge states due to ‘N’ and ‘S’ dopants as verified by Zeta potential results.

ce:section.ce:para: Intensive research has already been carried out to develop optical properties in graphene oxide by functionalization technique for sensing [1] and optoelectronic [2] applications. Recently, Graphene quantum dot (GQD), an attractive material with lateral dimension <10nm, has shown wide applications in the fields of catalysis [3–5], sensors [6–9], optoelectronics [10,11] and biomedical imaging [12–15] because of its lower toxicity level and better biocompatibility and photostability. During the past few years, similar to many other luminescent quantum dot structures viz. semiconductor quantum dots [16–18], noble metal nanoclusters [19], carbon dots [20–24], and GQD [25,26] display new opportunities due to quantum confinement and highly reactive edge states and good water solubility. Recently, selective detection of nitro aromatics particularly 2,4,6 trinitrotoluene (TNT) and 2,4,6 trinitrophenol (TNP) is of great concern due to global terrorism. Different luminescent materials including carbon [27–31], graphene [32–34], poly aromatic hydrocarbons [35] and transition metal di-sulphides [36,37] quantum dots are being used for selective detection of nitroexplosives exploiting photoluminescence (PL) quenching effect. However, highly sensitive PL quenching with low detection limit is still lacking. Several strategies have been reported to improve these properties among which doping by hetero atoms are an elegant approach to achieve high selectivity.

Few reports are already available in the literature in which quantum dots are doped by heteroatom to improve the PL quenching effect however the efficiency in most cases is not satisfactory. The origin of PL quenching is the transition among the localized levels created due to dopants in the quantum dots. Therefore, to increase PL quenching efficiency and lowest detection limit of nitroexplosives, one possibility is to create large number of localized energy levels near the conduction band by increasing the doping effect. To explore this, in the present work, chemically synthesized GQDs are co-doped by nitrogen and sulphur to increase the number of localized levels to enhance the PL quenching effect. The as-synthesized nitrogen and sulphur co-doped GQD shows high efficiency (92%) in PL quenching effect using only 90μM TNP solution and extremely low detection limit (19.05ppb) is achieved accordingly. The details are reported in this paper.

High resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HRTEM) has been carried out to understand the morphology of ‘N’, ‘S’ doped and co-doped GQDs. Fig. 1 (a) shows the TEM image of the as-synthesized N,S-GQD from which it is seen that the quantum dots are well-dispersed, nearly spherical with diameter range 1–5nm and average size of quantum dots is ∼2nm. High resolution TEM image also shows the crystallinity of N, S-GQD with in plane lattice spacing of 0.24nm as shown in Fig. 1(b). It is also noticed that in case of individual ‘N’ or ‘S’ doped GQD, the particle size is larger compared to co-doped samples as shown in Figs. S1 and S2 of supporting information.

Fig. 1(c) shows the FT-IR spectra of N,S-GQD. A broad absorption peak at 3000–3500cm−1 corresponds to stretching vibration of OH and NH bonds.CO stretching vibration appears at 1640cm−1 and 1405cm−1 corresponds to CN bending vibration [40]. Two peaks at 1316cm−1 and 1212cm−1arise due to asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of COC and the peak at 1129cm−1 and 1004cm−1 indicate the stretching modes of COH and CN respectively [29]. The peak at 778cm−1 appears due to vibration of pyrrole ring and the peak at 635cm−1 is due to CS stretching vibration. C=S stretching frequency appears at 1325cm−1 [41].

Raman spectra of N,S-GQD in Fig. 1(d) show D and G bands at ∼1365cm−1 and ∼1597cm−1 respectively. The intensity ratio of D to G (ID/IG) bands of N,S-GQD is about ∼1.17. This high (ID/IG) value indicates that GQD contains large defect sites with partial disordered crystal structure [15].

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy is used to investigate the chemical structure of N,S-GQD. Fig. 2 (a) shows the full range XPS spectra of the as-synthesized material that contains four elements C (1S at 286eV), O (1S at 536eV),N (1S at 400eV) and S (2p and 2S at 163 and 227eV). The high resolution spectrum of C1S has also been deconvoluted into different single peaks. It is seen that the material consists of five different peaks at 283.9, 284.7, 286.5, 287.1 and 288.5eV for C-S-C, CC, CO, CNC and CO bonds respectively as shown in Fig. 2(b). The deconvoluted spectrum of N1s (Fig. S3) shows three different peaks at 399.1, 400.3, 401.5eV for pyridinic N type, pyrrolic N type and NH type bonds. The high resolution spectrum of S2p (Fig. S4) consists of two peaks at 163.1eV and 164.7eV for thiophene (2p 3/2 and 2p1/2). Thus the presence of CNC and CSC bonds confirm the doping of N and S hetero atoms into the graphene network during hydrothermal synthesis.

We have measured the Zeta potential for doped GQD to study the nature of their surfaces as well as their electron density. Zeta potential values for N,S-GQD, N-GQD, S-GQD and GQD are found to be −22.1mV, −14.8mV, −15.5mV and −4.66mV respectively indicating all quantum dots bears negatively charged surfaces. Among them, N,S-GQD (Fig. 3 ) shows the highest negative value due to co-doping of both electron rich elements (N and S) compared to single doped or un-doped GQD. The figures of Zeta potentials for N-GQD, S-GQD and GQD are given in the supporting information (Fig. S5).

UV–vis spectra of N,S-GQD show two absorption peaks at 330nm and 365nm as shown in the supporting information(Fig. S6). The peak at 330nm arises due to n→π* transition of CO and CN bonds and peak at 365nm is ascribed due to π→π* transition of graphene network.

The as-synthesized N,S-GQD material shows highly intense blue PL emission with peak at 405nm when excited at 320nm. It shows quite blue shifted PL spectra compared to normal GQDs as N-GQD and S-GQD show their peak intensities at 420 and 433nm respectively as shown in the supporting information. This N,S-GQD also shows the excitation dependent photoluminescence property. This is a general feature of the quantum dots. With the change in excitation wavelength from 300 to 420nm, the emission peak also shifts from 403 to 507nm with the highest intensity at 405nm as shown in Fig. 4 (a). The excitation dependent photoluminescence is observed as a result of surface defect states in N,S-GQD and particle size variation. Nitrogen and sulphur doping into graphene quantum dots form different new surface states near the conduction band of GQD and the transition from these states results excitation dependent photoluminescence in co-doped quantum dots [42]. The material also shows pH dependent PL property as shown in Fig. 4(b). It is seen that the material shows maximum intensity at pH 10 and with decrease in pH, the PL intensity drastically decreases. As the QDs contain different carboxylic groups on surface, the aggregation takes place by those carboxylic groups due to protonation in acidic medium, leading to fluorescence quenching [43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of nitroexplosives in aqueous medium
ce:section.ce:para: We have explored this PL property for sensing applications of nitro explosives. For this, we have performed fluorescence quenching titration experiments with addition of aqueous solution of different nitro explosives like TNP; 2,6-dinitro toluene (2, 6-DNT); 2,4-dinitro toluene (2,4-DNT); 1,4-dinitro benzene (1,4-DNB); 1,3-dinitro benzene (1,3-DNB); 1,2-dinitro benzene (1, 2-DNB); 4-nitro toluene (4-NT), 2-nitro toluene (2-NT) and nitrobenzene (NB) etc. gradually to the aqueous solution of N,S-GQDs.

From Fig. 5 (a) we have seen that after addition of only 20μL of TNP solution, quite large fluorescence quenching is observed immediately and it increases up to 92% after addition of 200μL TNP solution. This indicates strong sensing ability of the as-synthesized material towards highly explosive TNP. We have also added the same amount of other nitro explosives to this QD solution to measure its quenching ability. But, it shows quite weak quenching ability towards other nitro compounds as shown in Fig. 5(b).

Furthermore, we have analyzed these PL quenching efficiencies by Stern-Volmer equation after inner filter effect correction, I0/I=KSV [Q]+1, where I0 and I are fluorescence intensities before and after addition of nitro explosive, [Q] is the molar concentration of analyte added and KSV is the quenching constant (M−1). From Fig. 6 (a), it is seen that only TNP shows an exponential S-V curve whereas other nitro compounds show quite linear curve. We have calculated the values of KSV for all these nitro explosives as given in Table 1 , shows that KSV value for TNP is quite higher than that of other nitro compounds. This reflects strong affinity of as-synthesized N,S-GQD towards TNP.

We have also carried out fluorescence quenching titration experiment with N,S-GQD to check its selectivity towards TNP. For this experiment, we have first added 40μL of 4-NT solution stepwise to a fixed volume of N,S-GQD solution to access most of its binding sites followed by the addition of 20μL TNP solution to this mixture. Further, we have carried out the same process for five consecutive times to check the selectivity. Now, we have performed the same experiment with other nitro compounds followed by the addition of TNP. We have plotted the quenching efficiency against volume of analyst added for all the nitro explosives in Fig. 6(b). We have seen that all the other nitro explosives show quite weak PL quenching efficiency in spite of their initial addition to GQD soln. After addition of TNP to this mixture, PL quenching drastically increases and even slightly affected further addition of other nitro compounds in this mixture solution. These experimental results clearly confirm the selectivity of NS-GQD towards TNP in presence of other nitro compounds.

The detection limit for TNP is calculated from fluorescence quenching experiment of N,S-GQD in presence of lower concentration of TNP solution (Fig. 7 (a)). The intercept (log [TNP]) to X-axis is obtained from the linear plot between (Imax −Ic)/(Imax −Imin) and log[TNP] shown in Fig. 7(b), where Imax, Ic and Imin are the initial fluorescence intensity, intensity after addition of different amount of 1mM TNP solution and intensity at saturation point respectively. From this, the detection limit is calculated using formula, ([TNP]×MWTNP)/1000 and multiplying this with factor 109, here MWTNP is the molecular weight of TNP. Now, we get the detection limit value in ppb unit. The calculated detection limits for TNP is 19.05ppbusing N,S-GQD as sensor.

To investigate the origin of such strong PL quenching of N,S-GQD by TNP solution, we have also carried out this PL quenching experiment with the virgin GQD, N doped and S doped GQD samples. From Fig. 8 (a), we have seen that all these three samples show quite lower PL quenching efficiency than N,S-GQD after addition of 200μL of TNP soln to a fixed volume of these samples. The quenching efficiency is as follows: N,S-GQD>>N-GQD∼S-GQD>>GQD. As the hetero atoms (N and S) have higher electron density compared to C atom of virgin GQDs, they make this GQD (N,S-GQD) more electron rich by the doping process during its synthesis. This is also supported by Zeta potential values. As the TNP is extremely electron deficient among all the other nitro compounds, it is quickly to be attached with the N,S-GQD resulting such strong PL quenching via photo-induced electron transfer (PET) mechanism. As a result of N and S co-doping into the carbon network, some additional levels will be created near conduction band among which the transition has taken place. During the PET process, when TNP is gradually added to this quantum dot solution, the PL spectra are quenched and red shifted (∼50nm) as shown in Fig. 5(a). As the electron rich edge states increase significantly in case of co-doped samples due to reduction of particle size, the PET process is favourable in case of co-doped samples to achieve higher quenching efficiency. In case of virgin GQDs or individual N/S doping, this type of large red shift in peak position is not observed. This confirms the predominance of PET mechanism in case of N,S-GQD sample during this PL quenching process.

The selectivity of this co-doped material for TNP can also be explained by H-bonding and π-π stacking interactions. As TNP is highly acidic in nature, it readily releases proton in the solution resulting some protonation at N-units of GQDs. This helps to form strong H-bonding interaction between N-sites of GQD and picrate anions of TNP [35]. This kind of H-bonding interaction is absent in case of other nitro explosives like TNT; 2,6-DNT; 2,4-DNT and 4-NT as they are very weakly acidic in nature. Due to presence of 3 nitro groups, the phenyl ring of TNP is highly electron deficient. This involves into some face to face Л-Л stacking interactions with electron rich graphene network [35]. These two interactions results such higher selectivity of N,S-GQD towards TNP over other nitro explosives.

Apart from PET process, there is another contributing factor in this PL quenching process, i.e. fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism. If the emission band of the fluorophore overlaps effectively with the absorption band of the nitro compounds, then FRET mechanism will be operated from fluorophore to these nitro compounds resulting PL quenching. As the absorption band of TNP effectively overlaps with the emission band of N,S-GQD compared to other nitro compounds as shown in Fig. 8(b), FRET mechanism is more predominant in case of TNP. We have also compared the overlap integral values [J(λ)] for all the nitro compounds. From Table 2 , we can see that TNP shows the highest J(λ) value compared to other nitro compounds. This also guides such higher selectivity of NS-GQD towards TNP compared to other nitro compounds. To compare the FRET efficiency in TNP sensing, we have also calculated the J(λ) values for N-doped, S-doped and virgin GQDs. From Table 3 , it is seen that the J(λ) values are as follows NS-GQD>N-GQD>GQD∼S-GQD. So, predominant PET together with efficient FRET effectively guide such superior PL quenching efficiency during TNP sensing in case of N and S co-doped GQDs compared to only N or S doped and virgin GQDS.

In conclusion, nitrogen and sulphur co-doped graphene quantum dots are synthesized to investigate the doping effect during nitroexplosives sensing. We have achieved to increase the fluorescence quenching effect up to 92% using only 90μM TNP solution compared to their individual counterpart. The detection limit has been calculated to about 19.05 ppb.

Graphite powder and all nitro aromatics like TNP (2,4,6-trinitro phenol), NB (nitrobenzene), 1,2-DNB (1,2-dinitro benzene), 1,3-DNB (1,3-dinitro benzene), 1,4-DNB (1,4-dinitro benzene), 2,4-DNT (2,4-dinitro benzene), 2,6-DNT (2,6-dinitro benzene) etc. are purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and sulphuric acid (98wt%),sodium nitrate, sodium hydroxide, hydrogen peroxide (30%), urea, thiourea, sodium sulphide, ammonia solution (25%), potassium permanganate are purchased from Merck Chemicals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of GQD, N-GQD, S-GQD, N, S-GQD
ce:section.ce:para: To synthesize GQD, in the first step, Graphene oxide (GO) was synthesized from graphite powder by modified Hummers method [38,39].10mL of as-synthesized GO was dispersed in 25mL of distilled water and sonicated for 30min. The pH value of the resulting solution was kept at pH-8 using 5N NaOH solution. The mixture was then transferred into a Teflon coated stainless steel autoclave at 200°C for 15h. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was filtered through 0.22μm Millipore filter papers and dialysed through dialysis bag (1KDa) for 12h. For N-doped and S-doped GQD, we have used the precursor materials viz. Urea (200mg) and Na2S (60mg) respectively with GO solution keeping the other processes unaltered. However, nitrogen and sulphur co-doped graphene quantum dots N,S-GQD were synthesized from graphene oxide solution as shown in Scheme 1 . To synthesize N,S-GQD, 200mg of thiourea was mixed with sonicated GO solution and maintained at pH-8 using ammonia solution and following the same procedure.

3mL of N, S-GQD solution (1mg/ml) was taken in a quartz cell and excited at 325nm using a xenon source and the PL data were recorded upon successive addition of different amount of 1mM TNP solution. This experiment was carried out at pH7 medium using phosphate buffer solution and the experiment was repeated with other nitro aromatics under same condition.

To characterize the as synthesized quantum dots, UV–vis absorption spectra were carried out using a Cary UV 5000 spectrophotometer, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy(FT-IR) was investigated using a NICOLET MAGNA IR 750 system. The X-Ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out using OMICRON-0571 system. To determine the Size of quantum dots, Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) was carried out using JEOL-2011 high resolution Transmission Electron Microscope. Raman spectra were collected by a Micro-Raman spectrometer (Model JYT-6400). PTI Fluromax QM-400 spectroflurometer was used to record the photoluminescence spectra for all samples.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: TKM and D.D. acknowledge CSIR and CSIR-SPM respectively, for awarding their fellowships. S.K.S. acknowledges DST, project no. SR/S2/CMP-0097/2012

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.11.012.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article: Structures of nitro aromatic compounds, quantum yield calculations, UV–vis spectra of N,S-GQD, Excitation dependent photoluminescence study of GQD, and PL quenching titration experiments are given in Supporting Information.

Amphiphilic egg-derived carbon dots: rapid plasma fabrication, pyrolysis process, and multicolor printing patterns

Magnetic field effect corroborated with docking study to explore photoinduced electron transfer in drug- protein interaction

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Tapas Kumar Mondal received his M.Sc. in chemistry from Indian Institute of Technology Delhi (IIT Delhi) in 2014. He is currently a PhD student in Department of Materials Science, Indian Association for the Cultivation of Science, Kolkata. His research interest is focused on the synthesis and characterization of highly luminescent carbon based quantum dots for sensing application.

Diptiman Dinda completed M.Sc. in chemistry from West Bengal State University. He has received Ph.D. degree in Chemistry from Indian Association for the Cultivation of Science. His main research interest includes designing of nano materials for opto-electronic as well as water splitting applications.

Shyamal K Saha studied physics from University of Calcutta and did Ph.D. in Indian Association for the Cultivation of Science. He is now professor in department of materials Science, Indian Association for the Cultivation of Science, Kolkata, India. His current research interests focus on the study of functional nanostructures particularly on 2D materials and quantum dots systems.
